title: Getting Started with bridgr
text: bridgr covers classic bridge equations, MIDAS-style mixed-frequency regressions, intermediate specifications estimate within-period weights data, behind single workflow bridging high-frequency indicators lower-frequency target. core workflow always : Provide one lower-frequency target series one higher-frequency indicators. Decide missing indicator observations handled indic_predict. Decide indicators aligned target frequency indic_aggregators. Fit target equation mf_model(), inspect summary(), produce target-period forecasts forecast(). vignette walks workflow package’s built-Swiss GDP indicator data.

title: Getting Started with bridgr
text: gdp_growth quarterly, baro monthly. mf_model() recognizes frequency mismatch automatically aligns indicator target frequency fitting target equation.

title: Getting Started with bridgr
text: bridgr assumes series submit already scale suitable linear regression — typically growth rates, differences, similarly stabilized transformations. package automatically re-transform inputs, can enable lightweight pre-fit diagnostic stationarity = "warn". example shows warning level GDP series confirms disappears differencing growth rates.
code: gdp_level_recent <- dplyr::slice_tail(gdp, n = 40) baro_recent <- dplyr::slice_tail(baro, n = 120)  invisible(tryCatch(   mf_model(     target = gdp_level_recent,     indic = baro_recent,     indic_predict = "last",     indic_aggregators = "mean",     h = 1,     stationarity = "warn"   ),   warning = function(w) message("Warning captured: ", conditionMessage(w)) )) #> Warning captured: Heuristic stationarity checks flagged target series `gdp_level_recent` (KPSS differencing signal). Consider differences, growth rates, log changes, demeaning, or other variance-stabilizing transformations before fitting.  stationary_model <- mf_model(   target = gdp_growth,   indic = baro,   indic_predict = "last",   indic_aggregators = "mean",   h = 1,   stationarity = "warn" )

title: Getting Started with bridgr
text: default "mean" aggregator classic bridge-model setup: monthly block completed first, averaged quarterly frequency target equation estimated. fitted object stores aligned data went estimation future target-period regressor path used forecasting.
code: bridge_model <- mf_model(   target = gdp_growth,   indic = baro,   indic_predict = "auto.arima",   indic_aggregators = "mean",   indic_lags = 1,   target_lags = 1,   h = 2 )  forecast(bridge_model) #> Mixed-frequency forecast #> ----------------------------------- #> Target series: gdp_growth #> Forecast horizon: 2 #> Uncertainty: point forecast only #> ----------------------------------- #>   time       mean  #> 1 2023-01-01 0.875 #> 2 2023-04-01 0.678 tail(bridge_model$estimation_set) #> # A tibble: 6 × 5 #>   time       gdp_growth  baro baro_lag1 gdp_growth_lag1 #>   <date>          <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl>           <dbl> #> 1 2021-07-01      2.34  112.      125.            2.47  #> 2 2021-10-01      0.411 104.      112.            2.34  #> 3 2022-01-01      0.105  97.4     104.            0.411 #> 4 2022-04-01      1.03   94.3      97.4           0.105 #> 5 2022-07-01      0.255  90.0      94.3           1.03  #> 6 2022-10-01      0.102  90.7      90.0           0.255 bridge_model$forecast_set #> # A tibble: 2 × 4 #>   time        baro baro_lag1 gdp_growth_lag1 #>   <date>     <dbl>     <dbl> <list>          #> 1 2023-01-01  97.4      90.7 <dbl [1]>       #> 2 2023-04-01  99.8      97.4 <dbl [1]>

title: Getting Started with bridgr
text: target_lags = p adds p autoregressive lags target right-hand side regression. indic_lags = q adds q lags aggregated indicator (frequency alignment). default 0. example target equation \[ \Delta y_t = \alpha   + \rho \, \Delta y_{t-1}   + \beta_0 \, \bar{x}_t   + \beta_1 \, \bar{x}_{t-1}   + \varepsilon_t, \] \(\bar{x}_t\) within-quarter mean monthly indicator. Forecasts beyond first horizon produced recursively, simulated target lags taking place observed ones -sample history exhausted.

title: Getting Started with bridgr
text: mf_model() accepts number indicators single long-format table. Indicators may share frequency come different frequencies, case one aligned target separately. example pairs level KOF barometer year--year change two monthly indicators. indicators aggregated independently enter final target equation separate regressors.
code: baro_yoy <- baro |>   dplyr::arrange(time) |>   dplyr::mutate(values = values - dplyr::lag(values, 12)) |>   dplyr::filter(!is.na(values))  indic_multi <- dplyr::bind_rows(   dplyr::mutate(baro, id = "baro_level"),   dplyr::mutate(baro_yoy, id = "baro_yoy") ) |>   dplyr::select(id, time, values)  multi_model <- mf_model(   target = gdp_growth,   indic = indic_multi,   indic_predict = c("last", "last"),   indic_aggregators = c("mean", "mean"),   target_lags = 1,   h = 1 )  multi_model$regressor_names #> [1] "baro_level"      "baro_yoy"        "gdp_growth_lag1" forecast(multi_model) #> Mixed-frequency forecast #> ----------------------------------- #> Target series: gdp_growth #> Forecast horizon: 1 #> Uncertainty: point forecast only #> ----------------------------------- #>   time       mean   #> 1 2023-01-01 -0.324

title: Getting Started with bridgr
text: set indic_predict = "direct", bridgr switches indicator forecasting direct alignment based observed complete high-frequency blocks. case, latest complete blocks assigned backward target periods instead forecast forward first, averaged within target period. particularly useful ragged edge want work observed high-frequency information avoid separate indicator forecasting step.
code: direct_model <- mf_model(   target = gdp_growth,   indic = baro,   indic_predict = "direct",   h = 1 )  forecast(direct_model) #> Mixed-frequency forecast #> ----------------------------------- #> Target series: gdp_growth #> Forecast horizon: 1 #> Uncertainty: point forecast only #> ----------------------------------- #>   time       mean  #> 1 2023-01-01 0.483

title: Getting Started with bridgr
text: default package uses regular frequency ladder (second → minute → hour → day → week → month → quarter → year) conventional conversion factors 60 seconds per minute, 24 hours per day, 3 months per quarter. data follow non-standard calendar — example 5-day business week 50 working weeks per year — pass named numeric vector frequency_conversions override defaults. See ?mf_model full set recognized names.

title: Getting Started with bridgr
text: default, bridgr returns point forecasts . want uncertainty output, request estimation time se = TRUE , needed, custom simulation full-system bootstrap controls bootstrap.  uncertainty implementation uses HAC standard errors linear target equation, Delta-HAC standard errors parametric aggregation weights estimated jointly. default, prediction intervals simulated resampled centered target-equation residuals. also set full_system_bootstrap = TRUE, bridgr instead uses full-system target-period block bootstrap coefficient standard errors prediction intervals, controlled bootstrap = list(N = ..., block_length = ...).
code: uncertainty_model <- mf_model(   target = gdp_growth,   indic = baro,   indic_predict = "auto.arima",   indic_aggregators = "mean",   target_lags = 1,   h = 4,   se = TRUE,   bootstrap = list(N = 40, block_length = NULL) )  forecast(uncertainty_model) #> Mixed-frequency forecast #> ----------------------------------- #> Target series: gdp_growth #> Forecast horizon: 4 #> Uncertainty: prediction intervals from residual resampling #> Simulation paths: 40 #> ----------------------------------- #>   time       mean  se    lower_80 upper_80 lower_95 upper_95 #> 1 2023-01-01 0.259 0.742 -0.804   0.975    -1.085   2.423    #> 2 2023-04-01 0.486 0.788 -0.593   1.750    -0.891   1.939    #> 3 2023-07-01 0.500 0.784 -0.601   1.422    -0.961   2.522    #> 4 2023-10-01 0.521 0.865 -0.579   1.626    -0.838   2.742 summary(uncertainty_model) #> Mixed-frequency model summary #> ----------------------------------- #> Target series: gdp_growth #> Target frequency: quarter #> Forecast horizon: 4 #> Estimation rows: 74 #> Regressors: baro, gdp_growth_lag1 #> ----------------------------------- #> Target equation coefficients: #>                 Estimate HAC SE #> (Intercept)      -10.988  3.304 #> baro               0.116  0.033 #> gdp_growth_lag1   -0.316  0.120 #> ----------------------------------- #> Model fit: #>  Statistic               Value #>  R-squared               0.572 #>  Adjusted R-squared      0.560 #>  Residual standard error 0.886 #> ----------------------------------- #> Indicator summary: #>      Frequency Predict    Aggregation #> baro month     auto.arima mean        #> ----------------------------------- #> Uncertainty: #> Coefficient SEs: hac #> Prediction intervals: residual resampling #> Simulation paths: 40 #> ----------------------------------- plot(uncertainty_model, type = "forecast")

title: Getting Started with bridgr
text: vignette vignette("mixed-frequency-modeling", package = "bridgr") compares main aggregation strategies shows bridgr moves classic bridge models unrestricted parametric MIDAS-style specifications. vignette vignette("ragged-edge-nowcasting", package = "bridgr") focuses indic_predict different ways handle incomplete high-frequency data forecast origin. vignette vignette("uncertainty--scenarios", package = "bridgr") shows work HAC / Delta-HAC coefficient uncertainty, residual-resampling prediction intervals, optional full-system bootstrap, scenario forecasts based custom future regressor paths.

title: Aggregation and Mixed-Frequency Modeling in bridgr
text: key design choice bridgr classic bridge equations, unrestricted mixed-frequency regressions, parametric MIDAS-style models share estimation, forecasting, summary workflow. can move without switching different API. bridgr supports several ways map higher-frequency observations lower-frequency target equation. choices span continuum: Bridge-style deterministic aggregation: "mean", "last", "sum", fixed numeric weight vector. Unrestricted mixed-frequency regression: "unrestricted". Parametric MIDAS-style weighting: "expalmon" "beta". vignette uses simple monthly--quarterly simulation differences approaches easy see.

title: Aggregation and Mixed-Frequency Modeling in bridgr
text: data-generating process intentionally driven middle month quarter — within-quarter weights (0.2, 0.6, 0.2) — can see different aggregation schemes react. monthly indicator also slot-specific within-quarter movements, unrestricted specification can estimate three distinct monthly coefficients.
code: n_quarters <- 40 quarter_index <- rep(seq_len(n_quarters), each = 3) slot <- rep(1:3, times = n_quarters) monthly_time <- seq(   as.Date("2010-01-01"),   by = "month",   length.out = n_quarters * 3 ) monthly_indicator <- dplyr::tibble(   time = monthly_time,   value = 15 + quarter_index * 0.35 +     ifelse(slot == 1, 0.8 * sin(quarter_index / 2), 0) +     ifelse(slot == 2, -0.6 * cos(quarter_index / 3), 0) +     ifelse(slot == 3, 0.7 * sin(quarter_index / 4 + 0.3), 0) )  quarter_time <- monthly_time[seq(1, length(monthly_time), by = 3)] quarter_target <- dplyr::tibble(   time = quarter_time,   value = 0.5 +     vapply(       seq_along(quarter_time),       function(i) {         block <- monthly_indicator$value[((i - 1) * 3 + 1):(i * 3)]         0.2 * block[[1]] + 0.6 * block[[2]] + 0.2 * block[[3]]       },       numeric(1)     ) +     rep(c(0.1, -0.05, 0.08, -0.02), length.out = length(quarter_time)) )

title: Aggregation and Mixed-Frequency Modeling in bridgr
text: classic bridge-model choices. easy interpret often work well want transparent rule within-period aggregation.
code: mean_model <- mf_model(   target = quarter_target,   indic = monthly_indicator,   indic_predict = "last",   indic_aggregators = "mean",   h = 1 )  last_model <- mf_model(   target = quarter_target,   indic = monthly_indicator,   indic_predict = "last",   indic_aggregators = "last",   h = 1 )  summary(mean_model) #> Mixed-frequency model summary #> ----------------------------------- #> Target series: quarter_target #> Target frequency: quarter #> Forecast horizon: 1 #> Estimation rows: 40 #> Regressors: monthly_indicator #> ----------------------------------- #> Target equation coefficients: #>                   Estimate #> (Intercept)          0.516 #> monthly_indicator    0.999 #> ----------------------------------- #> Model fit: #>  Statistic               Value #>  R-squared               0.999 #>  Adjusted R-squared      0.999 #>  Residual standard error 0.142 #> ----------------------------------- #> Indicator summary: #>                   Frequency Predict Aggregation #> monthly_indicator month     last    mean        #> -----------------------------------

title: Aggregation and Mixed-Frequency Modeling in bridgr
text: already prior within-period shape, pass numeric weight vector list() instead. Weights must right length inferred target-period block size must sum one.
code: fixed_model <- mf_model(   target = quarter_target,   indic = monthly_indicator,   indic_predict = "last",   indic_aggregators = list(c(0.1, 0.7, 0.2)),   h = 1 )  stats::coef(fixed_model) #>       (Intercept) monthly_indicator  #>         0.5676102         0.9984672

title: Aggregation and Mixed-Frequency Modeling in bridgr
text: "unrestricted" estimates one coefficient within-quarter monthly observation. monthly--quarterly example means three separate regressors. Notice recovered coefficients close true (0.2, 0.6, 0.2) weights used simulated DGP — second slot dominates, designed. example keeps indic_predict = "last" comparison isolates aggregation choice. ragged-edge vignette covers indic_predict = "direct", skips indicator forecasting instead works latest observed complete high-frequency blocks.
code: unrestricted_model <- mf_model(   target = quarter_target,   indic = monthly_indicator,   indic_predict = "last",   indic_aggregators = "unrestricted",   h = 1 )  stats::coef(unrestricted_model) #>           (Intercept) monthly_indicator_hf1 monthly_indicator_hf2  #>             0.5411254             0.1983962             0.5961748  #> monthly_indicator_hf3  #>             0.2047900

title: Aggregation and Mixed-Frequency Modeling in bridgr
text: bridgr also supports parametric weighting rules estimate within-period shape data keeping number free parameters small. solver_options list controls optimizer — see ?mf_model full set available controls. fitted object stores estimated weight profile underlying parametric coefficients.  parametric profiles concentrate mass middle month, matching simulated DGP. fixed "mean" rule spreads mass evenly across months "last" puts final month.
code: expalmon_model <- mf_model(   target = quarter_target,   indic = monthly_indicator,   indic_predict = "last",   indic_aggregators = "expalmon",   solver_options = list(seed = 123, n_starts = 1, maxiter = 100),   h = 1 )  beta_model <- mf_model(   target = quarter_target,   indic = monthly_indicator,   indic_predict = "last",   indic_aggregators = "beta",   solver_options = list(     seed = 123,     n_starts = 1,     maxiter = 100,     start_values = c(2, 2)   ),   h = 1 ) indicator_id <- expalmon_model$indic_name[[1]]  equal_weights <- rep(1 / 3, 3) last_weights <- c(0, 0, 1) true_weights <- c(0.2, 0.6, 0.2)  weights_df <- dplyr::bind_rows(   dplyr::tibble(model = "mean",        month = 1:3, weight = equal_weights),   dplyr::tibble(model = "last",        month = 1:3, weight = last_weights),   dplyr::tibble(model = "true DGP",    month = 1:3, weight = true_weights),   dplyr::tibble(model = "expalmon",    month = 1:3,                 weight = expalmon_model$parametric_weights[[indicator_id]]),   dplyr::tibble(model = "beta",        month = 1:3,                 weight = beta_model$parametric_weights[[indicator_id]]) )  ggplot2::ggplot(   weights_df,   ggplot2::aes(x = .data$month, y = .data$weight, color = .data$model) ) +   ggplot2::geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   ggplot2::geom_point(size = 2) +   ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:3) +   ggplot2::labs(     title = "Within-quarter weight profiles",     x = "Month within quarter",     y = "Weight"   ) +   theme_bridgr()

title: Aggregation and Mixed-Frequency Modeling in bridgr
text: models share downstream interface.
code: forecasts_df <- dplyr::bind_rows(   dplyr::tibble(model = "mean",                 forecast = as.numeric(forecast(mean_model)$mean)),   dplyr::tibble(model = "last",                 forecast = as.numeric(forecast(last_model)$mean)),   dplyr::tibble(model = "unrestricted",                 forecast = as.numeric(forecast(unrestricted_model)$mean)),   dplyr::tibble(model = "expalmon",                 forecast = as.numeric(forecast(expalmon_model)$mean)),   dplyr::tibble(model = "beta",                 forecast = as.numeric(forecast(beta_model)$mean)) )  ggplot2::ggplot(   forecasts_df,   ggplot2::aes(x = .data$model, y = .data$forecast, fill = .data$model) ) +   ggplot2::geom_col(width = 0.6, show.legend = FALSE) +   ggplot2::labs(     title = "One-step-ahead forecast by aggregation strategy",     x = NULL, y = "Forecast"   ) +   theme_bridgr()

what: Picking an Aggregator on Out-of-Sample Performance
title: Aggregation and Mixed-Frequency Modeling in bridgr
text: rough rule--thumb guide aggregator choice given end vignette. practice best way choose evaluate candidate models --sample forecasts. example uses short pseudo-real-time rolling-origin scheme simulated data, one-quarter-ahead forecasts. Even small example unrestricted parametric specifications recover within-quarter shape outperform deterministic mean. real application typically use longer evaluation window, multiple horizons, richer scoring rules.
code: origins <- (n_quarters - 13):(n_quarters - 4) methods <- c("mean", "unrestricted", "expalmon")  eval_rows <- list() for (m in methods) {   for (origin in origins) {     train <- dplyr::slice(quarter_target, seq_len(origin))     truth <- quarter_target$value[[origin + 1]]     fit <- mf_model(       target = train,       indic = monthly_indicator,       indic_predict = "last",       indic_aggregators = m,       h = 1,       solver_options = if (m == "expalmon") {         list(seed = 1, n_starts = 1, maxiter = 100)       } else {         NULL       }     )     fc <- as.numeric(forecast(fit)$mean)[[1]]     eval_rows[[length(eval_rows) + 1]] <- dplyr::tibble(       method = m, origin = origin, forecast = fc, actual = truth     )   } }  eval_df <- dplyr::bind_rows(eval_rows) eval_summary <- eval_df |>   dplyr::group_by(.data$method) |>   dplyr::summarise(     rmse = sqrt(mean((.data$forecast - .data$actual)^2)),     mae = mean(abs(.data$forecast - .data$actual)),     .groups = "drop"   )  eval_summary #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   method         rmse    mae #>   <chr>         <dbl>  <dbl> #> 1 expalmon     0.0701 0.0686 #> 2 mean         0.107  0.0870 #> 3 unrestricted 0.0701 0.0686

title: Aggregation and Mixed-Frequency Modeling in bridgr
text: rough guide: Use deterministic bridge aggregation like "mean", "last", "sum" want transparent stable nowcasting rule. Use "unrestricted" frequency gap small want within-period observation coefficient. Use "expalmon" "beta" want data-driven within-period weights like parsimonious parameterization "unrestricted". Use indic_predict = "direct" want direct alignment based latest observed complete high-frequency blocks.

title: Aggregation and Mixed-Frequency Modeling in bridgr
text: used midasr, closest bridgr analogues MIDAS regressors indic_aggregators = "expalmon" indic_aggregators = "beta". "unrestricted" aggregator plays role similar midasr’s U-MIDAS specification. Compared midasml, targets high-dimensional regularized mixed-frequency models, bridgr aimed lower-dimensional applied forecasting workflows.

title: Ragged-Edge Nowcasting with bridgr
text: Mixed-frequency nowcasting usually happens ragged edge: lower-frequency target released yet, higher-frequency indicators partially observed current target period. bridgr handles situation indic_predict, controls missing high-frequency tail completed target equation estimated forecast. currently supported options : "last": extend latest available high-frequency observation. "mean": extend mean latest available high-frequency block. "auto.arima": fit automatic ARIMA model indicator forecast missing observations. "ets": fit ETS model indicator forecast missing observations. "direct": forecast indicator . Instead, align latest observed complete high-frequency blocks directly target periods average within target period.

title: Ragged-Edge Nowcasting with bridgr
text: start package’s quarterly GDP growth series monthly barometer, remove last quarterly GDP observation last two monthly indicator observations. creates clean nowcast setup: final quarter now forecasted rather estimated, monthly indicator partially observed forecast quarter. target history now ends one quarter earlier, monthly indicator contains partial block quarter want nowcast.

title: Ragged-Edge Nowcasting with bridgr
text: model specification case. difference missing monthly observations completed indicator aggregated quarterly frequency.
code: predict_methods <- c("last", "mean", "auto.arima", "ets")  models <- lapply(   predict_methods,   function(method) {     mf_model(       target = gdp_nowcast,       indic = baro_ragged,       indic_predict = method,       indic_aggregators = "mean",       target_lags = 1,       h = 1     )   } ) names(models) <- predict_methods  forecast_df <- dplyr::bind_rows(   lapply(predict_methods, function(method) {     fc <- forecast(models[[method]])     dplyr::tibble(       method = method,       forecast_time = fc$time,       forecast = as.numeric(fc$mean)     )   }) )  forecast_df #> # A tibble: 4 × 3 #>   method     forecast_time forecast #>   <chr>      <date>           <dbl> #> 1 last       2022-10-01      -0.968 #> 2 mean       2022-10-01      -0.876 #> 3 auto.arima 2022-10-01      -0.706 #> 4 ets        2022-10-01      -0.968

title: Ragged-Edge Nowcasting with bridgr
text: plot shows tail observed monthly indicator together two extrapolated months indic_predict rule produces partially observed forecast quarter.  differences across rules exactly names suggest: "last" holds last value flat, "mean" extends mean recent block, auto.arima / ets produce model-based paths.
code: last_obs <- max(baro_ragged$time) last_obs_value <- baro_ragged$values[baro_ragged$time == last_obs] future_months <- seq(last_obs, by = "month", length.out = 3)[-1]  observed_tail <- baro_ragged |>   dplyr::slice_tail(n = 9) |>   dplyr::transmute(     time = .data$time,     value = .data$values,     method = "observed"   )  extended <- dplyr::bind_rows(   lapply(predict_methods, function(method) {     indicator_tbl <- models[[method]]$indic     extension <- indicator_tbl |>       dplyr::filter(.data$time %in% future_months) |>       dplyr::transmute(         time = .data$time,         value = .data$values,         method = method       )     # join to the last observed point for a continuous line     dplyr::bind_rows(       dplyr::tibble(time = last_obs, value = last_obs_value, method = method),       extension     )   }) )  ggplot2::ggplot(   dplyr::bind_rows(observed_tail, extended),   ggplot2::aes(x = .data$time, y = .data$value, color = .data$method) ) +   ggplot2::geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   ggplot2::geom_point(size = 1.6) +   ggplot2::labs(     title = "Indicator completion at the ragged edge",     x = NULL,     y = "KOF barometer",     color = NULL   ) +   theme_bridgr()

title: Ragged-Edge Nowcasting with bridgr
text: "direct" takes different route. indicator forecasted. Instead, bridgr assigns latest complete high-frequency blocks backward target periods averages within target period. especially useful want avoid separate indicator forecasting step prefer work observed high-frequency data.
code: direct_model <- mf_model(   target = gdp_nowcast,   indic = baro_ragged,   indic_predict = "direct",   h = 1 )  forecast(direct_model) #> Mixed-frequency forecast #> ----------------------------------- #> Target series: gdp_nowcast #> Forecast horizon: 1 #> Uncertainty: point forecast only #> ----------------------------------- #>   time       mean   #> 1 2022-10-01 -0.150

title: Ragged-Edge Nowcasting with bridgr
text: Real-time indicator data sometimes contain explicit NA cells inside sample, example late releases reporting holidays. Implicit ragged edges (shorter indicator tail NA cells) always supported indic_predict. default bridgr errors explicit NA values; missing argument controls instead. missing = "impute" interpolates linearly inside series generally useful choice. example introduces single internal gap re-fits model "error" (default, captured ) "impute". missing = "drop" removes NA rows entirely useful rows break per-period completeness required inferred frequency ladder — example sit beyond estimation sample. ragged-edge workflows indic_predict better mechanism handling trailing gaps.
code: baro_internal_na <- baro_ragged internal_gap <- seq(nrow(baro_internal_na) - 14, nrow(baro_internal_na) - 13) baro_internal_na$values[internal_gap] <- NA_real_  invisible(tryCatch(   mf_model(     target = gdp_nowcast,     indic = baro_internal_na,     indic_predict = "last",     indic_aggregators = "mean",     h = 1   ),   error = function(e) message("Error captured: ", conditionMessage(e)) )) #> Error captured: `indic` contains missing values.  imputed_model <- mf_model(   target = gdp_nowcast,   indic = baro_internal_na,   indic_predict = "last",   indic_aggregators = "mean",   h = 1,   missing = "impute" ) #> Warning in mf_model(target = gdp_nowcast, indic = baro_internal_na, #> indic_predict = "last", : `indic` contains 2 missing values; imputing them by #> linear interpolation within each series.  forecast(imputed_model) #> Mixed-frequency forecast #> ----------------------------------- #> Target series: gdp_nowcast #> Forecast horizon: 1 #> Uncertainty: point forecast only #> ----------------------------------- #>   time       mean   #> 1 2022-10-01 -0.897

title: Ragged-Edge Nowcasting with bridgr
text: rough guide: Use "last" simple carry-forward rule acceptable. Use "mean" want stable deterministic fill based latest high-frequency block. Use "auto.arima" "ets" indicator meaningful time-series structure separate extrapolation step sensible. Use "direct" want direct alignment without indicator forecasting want rely latest observed complete high-frequency blocks. good part downstream workflow change. summary() forecast() interface works across nowcasting choices.

title: A Real-Time Nowcasting Exercise
text: practice, GDP nowcasts produced repeatedly quarter new monthly indicator data arrives. useful nowcast two things : improves target quarter unfolds indicator data becomes available. beats simple benchmarks built past target observations. vignette walks exercise package’s Swiss data, combining quarterly GDP growth monthly KOF barometer. reproduces canonical real-time pattern — accuracy improves information set — compares three aggregation strategies naive sample-mean benchmark.

title: A Real-Time Nowcasting Exercise
text: target_q evaluation window want three nowcast vintages: Vintage 0: start target_q, monthly observations inside target_q yet available. Vintage 1: one month target_q, first monthly observation available. Vintage 2: two months , first two monthly observations available. vintage uses target history target_q - 1 corresponding indicator slice. use indic_predict = "last" extrapolate remaining within-quarter slots cheap, deterministic way; auto.arima work higher cost.
code: gdp_growth <- suppressMessages(tsbox::ts_na_omit(tsbox::ts_pc(gdp)))  # Evaluation window: the most recent 10 completed quarters. n_q <- nrow(gdp_growth) eval_indices <- (n_q - 11):(n_q - 2) eval_quarters <- gdp_growth$time[eval_indices] range(eval_quarters) #> [1] "2020-01-01" "2022-04-01" fit_at_vintage <- function(target_q, vintage, aggregator) {   train_target <- gdp_growth |>     dplyr::filter(.data$time < target_q)    cutoff_month <- target_q %m+% lubridate::period(num = vintage, units = "month")   train_indic <- baro |>     dplyr::filter(.data$time < cutoff_month)    solver <- if (aggregator == "expalmon") {     list(seed = 1, n_starts = 1, maxiter = 50)   } else {     NULL   }    mf_model(     target = train_target,     indic = train_indic,     indic_predict = "last",     indic_aggregators = aggregator,     target_lags = 1,     h = 1,     solver_options = solver   ) }

title: A Real-Time Nowcasting Exercise
text: running full loop, look nowcast one quarter evolves across vintages.  move vintage 0 vintage 2 methods generally pull toward realized value monthly information target quarter becomes available.
code: demo_q <- eval_quarters[length(eval_quarters) - 2] demo_truth <- gdp_growth$values[gdp_growth$time == demo_q]  demo_methods <- c("mean", "unrestricted", "expalmon") demo_rows <- list() for (m in demo_methods) {   for (v in 0:2) {     fit <- fit_at_vintage(demo_q, v, m)     fc <- as.numeric(forecast(fit)$mean)[[1]]     demo_rows[[length(demo_rows) + 1]] <- dplyr::tibble(       method = m, vintage = v, forecast = fc     )   } } demo_df <- dplyr::bind_rows(demo_rows)  ggplot2::ggplot(   demo_df,   ggplot2::aes(x = .data$vintage, y = .data$forecast, color = .data$method) ) +   ggplot2::geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   ggplot2::geom_point(size = 2) +   ggplot2::geom_hline(     yintercept = demo_truth,     linetype = "dashed",     color = "grey40"   ) +   ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(breaks = 0:2) +   ggplot2::labs(     title = paste0(       "Nowcasting ", format(demo_q, "%Y Q%q"),       " across three vintages (dashed = realized value)"     ),     x = "Vintage (months of indicator observed inside the target quarter)",     y = "GDP growth forecast"   ) +   theme_bridgr()

title: A Real-Time Nowcasting Exercise
text: Now iterate evaluation window, three vintages, three aggregators.
code: methods <- c("mean", "unrestricted", "expalmon") results <- list()  for (q_idx in seq_along(eval_quarters)) {   target_q <- eval_quarters[[q_idx]]   truth <- gdp_growth$values[gdp_growth$time == target_q]   for (v in 0:2) {     for (m in methods) {       fit <- fit_at_vintage(target_q, v, m)       fc <- as.numeric(forecast(fit)$mean)[[1]]       results[[length(results) + 1]] <- dplyr::tibble(         target_q = target_q,         vintage = v,         method = m,         forecast = fc,         actual = truth,         error = fc - truth       )     }   } }  # Sample-mean benchmark: prevailing-mean of in-sample target growth. for (q_idx in seq_along(eval_quarters)) {   target_q <- eval_quarters[[q_idx]]   truth <- gdp_growth$values[gdp_growth$time == target_q]   bench <- mean(gdp_growth$values[gdp_growth$time < target_q])   for (v in 0:2) {     results[[length(results) + 1]] <- dplyr::tibble(       target_q = target_q,       vintage = v,       method = "sample mean",       forecast = bench,       actual = truth,       error = bench - truth     )   } }  results_df <- dplyr::bind_rows(results)

title: A Real-Time Nowcasting Exercise
text: clearest single chart RMSE vintage, method separate line.  Two patterns typical setup: Accuracy improves information set. three indicator- based methods see RMSE fall vintage advances, sample-mean benchmark flat — use indicator information . Method ranking sensitive vintage. vintage 0 simple mean aggregator often competitive within-quarter shape fully extrapolated parametric weights exploit information yet observed. vintages 1 2 unrestricted parametric specifications start differentiate within-quarter behavior.
code: scoreboard <- results_df |>   dplyr::group_by(.data$method, .data$vintage) |>   dplyr::summarise(     rmse = sqrt(mean(.data$error^2)),     mae = mean(abs(.data$error)),     .groups = "drop"   ) |>   dplyr::arrange(.data$method, .data$vintage)  scoreboard #> # A tibble: 12 × 4 #>    method       vintage  rmse   mae #>    <chr>          <int> <dbl> <dbl> #>  1 expalmon           0  3.32  2.10 #>  2 expalmon           1  2.61  1.79 #>  3 expalmon           2  2.56  1.91 #>  4 mean               0  3.33  2.10 #>  5 mean               1  2.57  1.77 #>  6 mean               2  2.57  1.92 #>  7 sample mean        0  3.21  2.05 #>  8 sample mean        1  3.21  2.05 #>  9 sample mean        2  3.21  2.05 #> 10 unrestricted       0  3.35  2.14 #> 11 unrestricted       1  2.77  1.85 #> 12 unrestricted       2  2.72  1.97 ggplot2::ggplot(   scoreboard,   ggplot2::aes(x = .data$vintage, y = .data$rmse, color = .data$method) ) +   ggplot2::geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   ggplot2::geom_point(size = 2) +   ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(breaks = 0:2) +   ggplot2::labs(     title = "Nowcast RMSE by vintage",     x = "Vintage (months of indicator observed inside the target quarter)",     y = "RMSE",     color = NULL   ) +   theme_bridgr()

Points closer 45-degree line accurate forecasts. sample-mean benchmark sits horizontal cloud — react realized GDP movements . indicator-based methods spread along 45-degree line tighten vintage advances.

title: Uncertainty and Scenario Forecasting
text: bridgr can return point forecasts , can also compute coefficient uncertainty fitted target equation prediction intervals forecasts. relevant estimation arguments : se = FALSE: point forecasts . se = TRUE: compute HAC Delta-HAC coefficient uncertainty prediction intervals. bootstrap = list(N = 100, block_length = NULL): control number predictive simulation paths. block_length used full_system_bootstrap = TRUE, sets size contiguous target-period blocks resampled bootstrap draw — larger blocks preserve temporal dependence. full_system_bootstrap = TRUE: replace default residual-resampling prediction intervals HAC / Delta-HAC coefficient standard errors full-system target-period block bootstrap. refits full mixed-frequency workflow every draw, can substantially slower.

what: Fitting a Model with Default Uncertainty
title: Uncertainty and Scenario Forecasting
text: bridgr computes HAC standard errors linear target equation, Delta-HAC standard errors parametric aggregation weights estimated jointly. Forecast uncertainty obtained simulating resampled centered residuals fitted target equation.
code: gdp_growth <- suppressMessages(tsbox::ts_na_omit(tsbox::ts_pc(gdp)))  boot_model <- mf_model(   target = gdp_growth,   indic = baro,   indic_predict = "auto.arima",   indic_aggregators = "mean",   indic_lags = 1,   target_lags = 1,   h = 2,   se = TRUE,   bootstrap = list(N = 20, block_length = NULL) )

title: Uncertainty and Scenario Forecasting
text: model estimated se = TRUE, forecast() returns standardized forecast object : mean se lower upper forecast_set uncertainty metadata intervals empirical prediction intervals based stored residual-resampling forecast draws.
code: fc <- forecast(boot_model)  fc #> Mixed-frequency forecast #> ----------------------------------- #> Target series: gdp_growth #> Forecast horizon: 2 #> Uncertainty: prediction intervals from residual resampling #> Simulation paths: 20 #> ----------------------------------- #>   time       mean  se    lower_80 upper_80 lower_95 upper_95 #> 1 2023-01-01 0.875 0.742 -0.401   1.859    -0.522   2.120    #> 2 2023-04-01 0.678 0.598 0.035    1.623    -0.317   1.970 fc$bootstrap #> $requested #> [1] FALSE #>  #> $enabled #> [1] FALSE #>  #> $N #> [1] 20 #>  #> $valid_N #> [1] 0 #>  #> $block_length #> NULL

what: Reporting Only Forecasts Below a Width Tolerance
title: Uncertainty and Scenario Forecasting
text: practice prediction intervals widen horizon. common reporting choice publish forecast horizons whose interval narrower application-specific tolerance — example, publish 95% half-width chosen threshold. example applies rule tolerance 1.5 percentage points. Tighten relax tolerance depending much uncertainty acceptable use case.
code: forecast_table <- dplyr::tibble(   time = fc$time,   mean = as.numeric(fc$mean),   lower_95 = fc$lower[, "95%"],   upper_95 = fc$upper[, "95%"] ) |>   dplyr::mutate(     half_width_95 = (.data$upper_95 - .data$lower_95) / 2   )  tolerance <- 1.5 dplyr::filter(forecast_table, .data$half_width_95 <= tolerance) #> # A tibble: 2 × 5 #>   time        mean lower_95 upper_95 half_width_95 #>   <date>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>         <dbl> #> 1 2023-01-01 0.875   -0.522     2.12          1.32 #> 2 2023-04-01 0.678   -0.317     1.97          1.14

title: Uncertainty and Scenario Forecasting
text: want forecast target different future regressor path, pass custom xreg object forecast(). custom regressor names must match ones used fitted target equation. example defines baseline scenario (indicator continues model-implied path) negative shock scenario (indicator drops 5 points relative baseline). scenarios share fitted target equation uncertainty method.  Scenario forecasts reuse uncertainty method evaluate supplied regressor path.
code: make_xreg <- function(level_shift) {   dplyr::tibble(     id = rep(boot_model$xreg_names, each = nrow(boot_model$forecast_base_set)),     time = rep(       boot_model$forecast_base_set$time,       times = length(boot_model$xreg_names)     ),     value = c(       boot_model$forecast_base_set$baro + level_shift,       boot_model$forecast_base_set$baro_lag1 + level_shift     )   ) }  fc_baseline <- forecast(boot_model, xreg = make_xreg(0)) fc_shock    <- forecast(boot_model, xreg = make_xreg(-5))  scenario_df <- dplyr::bind_rows(   dplyr::tibble(     scenario = "baseline",     time = fc_baseline$time,     mean = as.numeric(fc_baseline$mean),     lower = fc_baseline$lower[, "95%"],     upper = fc_baseline$upper[, "95%"]   ),   dplyr::tibble(     scenario = "shock (-5)",     time = fc_shock$time,     mean = as.numeric(fc_shock$mean),     lower = fc_shock$lower[, "95%"],     upper = fc_shock$upper[, "95%"]   ) )  ggplot2::ggplot(   scenario_df,   ggplot2::aes(x = .data$time, color = .data$scenario, fill = .data$scenario) ) +   ggplot2::geom_ribbon(     ggplot2::aes(ymin = .data$lower, ymax = .data$upper),     alpha = 0.2, color = NA   ) +   ggplot2::geom_line(ggplot2::aes(y = .data$mean), linewidth = 0.8) +   ggplot2::geom_point(ggplot2::aes(y = .data$mean), size = 2) +   ggplot2::labs(     title = "Forecast under baseline and shock scenarios",     x = NULL, y = "GDP growth forecast"   ) +   theme_bridgr()

title: Uncertainty and Scenario Forecasting

code: full_model <- mf_model(   target = gdp_growth,   indic = baro,   indic_predict = "auto.arima",   indic_aggregators = "mean",   indic_lags = 1,   target_lags = 1,   h = 2,   se = TRUE,   full_system_bootstrap = TRUE,   bootstrap = list(N = 20, block_length = NULL) )  forecast(full_model)$bootstrap #> $requested #> [1] TRUE #>  #> $enabled #> [1] TRUE #>  #> $N #> [1] 20 #>  #> $valid_N #> [1] 20 #>  #> $block_length #> [1] 5

text: bridgr provides unified workflow bridging high-frequency indicators lower-frequency target series — practical job heart nowcasting forecasting macroeconomic variables GDP inflation. supports classical bridge equations well MIDAS-style mixed-frequency regressions (expalmon, beta, unrestricted / U-MIDAS) single interface, automatic frequency alignment, indicator forecasting, aggregation. typical workflow bridgr takes low-frequency target (e.g., quarterly GDP) one higher-frequency indicators (e.g., monthly surveys daily web data), forecasts missing indicator observations, aggregates target frequency, fits regression optional autoregressive target dynamics. makes possible produce timely predictions well official release low-frequency data — essential policymakers, central banks, applied forecasters need early reads macroeconomy. bridgr supports regular frequency ladders second year, several deterministic model-based indicator forecasting rules, multiple aggregation choices including joint expalmon beta weighting. target period contains high-frequency observations implied current frequency mapping, package keeps recent observations reports summarized warning. Compared midasr, broad toolkit estimating, testing, selecting, forecasting MIDAS regressions, bridgr focuses end--end nowcasting workflows automatic frequency alignment, indicator extension, aggregation, simpler interface raw series forecast. Compared midasml, targets high-dimensional mixed-frequency time-series panel models regularization methods sparse-group LASSO, bridgr aimed lower-dimensional applied forecasting settings transparent specification, interpretation, standard forecasting tooling priority.

title: bridgr (development version)
text: Rename main model-construction entry point mf_model(), rename fitted-model class S3 methods mf_model, keep bridge() deprecated compatibility wrapper. Extend mf_model() beyond classic bridge aggregation: add unrestricted mixed-frequency regressors via indic_aggregators = "unrestricted" add parametric "beta" weighting alongside "expalmon" add direct high-frequency alignment via indic_predict = "direct" support fixed numeric aggregation weights supplied list() Improve mixed-frequency input handling: infer regular frequencies second year allow custom frequency_conversions standardize month-, quarter-, year-end dates period starts needed frequency recognition keep recent observations overfilled target periods summarized warning fail target periods contain high-frequency observations Add joint parametric aggregation optimization controls solver_options, including optimizer choice, multi-start runs, seeds, iteration limits, user-supplied starting values. Add uncertainty support: se = TRUE coefficient uncertainty prediction intervals HAC standard errors linear bridge equations Delta-HAC standard errors parametric aggregation weights estimated jointly residual-resampling prediction intervals default optional full-system block bootstrap uncertainty via full_system_bootstrap = TRUE Add scenario forecasting support forecast.mf_model() custom future xreg paths standardized forecast objects uncertainty metadata. Add plotting methods helpers: plot.mf_model() fit forecast plots theme_bridgr(), colors_bridgr(), scale_color_bridgr(), scale_fill_bridgr() Expand printed output documentation: standardize summary.mf_model() forecast.mf_model() output add vignettes mixed-frequency modeling, ragged-edge nowcasting, uncertainty / scenario analysis refresh README examples package references Remove legendre parametric aggregation option. Use analytic gradients expalmon optimization improve normalized beta polynomial gradient used optimizer.
